questions.question: What is the main reason to use more than one cache layer?
options: Different layers can optimize for different distance, scope, and freshness needs.
Because every request must be cached in every possible place.
Because browsers cannot revalidate responses.
Because origin storage cannot serve immutable assets.
questions.explanation: Layered caching works when each layer has a distinct responsibility.

questions.question: What is the clearest danger in a shared CDN cache?
options: Serving personalized or tenant-specific data under a public cache key.
Reducing origin egress too much.
Returning headers to the client.
Improving latency for static assets.
questions.explanation: Key ambiguity in shared caches can create severe privacy or tenancy bugs.

questions.question: Why are immutable assets especially good for browser and CDN caching?
options: They can be cached aggressively with versioned names and minimal correctness risk.
They require purge on every request.
They cannot be compressed at the edge.
They only work in single-region systems.
questions.explanation: Versioned immutable assets are the easiest and safest wins in a cache hierarchy.

questions.question: Which metric best shows whether purge or invalidation is healthy across POPs?
options: Purge propagation latency or version skew by region.
Only total company revenue.
The number of markdown links in the lesson.
CPU model of developer laptops.
questions.explanation: Cross-region propagation timing directly measures whether mutable cached content is converging fast enough.

questions.question: What is the hidden debugging cost of layered caches?
options: A stale response may come from different layers, so headers and traces must reveal where it was served from.
Layered caches make traces impossible by design.
Only browsers need debugging, not CDNs.
More cache layers always simplify incident response.
questions.explanation: Without cache-debug visibility, operators cannot localize stale or leaking responses.

questions.question: Which response class is the safest default for a shared edge cache?
options: Public content with clear keys and cache-control rules.
Any authenticated response as long as it is popular.
All tenant-specific configuration.
Any response that ever returned 200 once.
questions.explanation: Shared caches are best for explicitly public content unless the keying model is extremely disciplined.

questions.question: Which interview answer is strongest?
options: I will cache the same payload identically in browser, CDN, and origin caches for simplicity.
I will classify content into immutable public, mutable public, and personalized paths, then assign each layer a clear role and cache key policy.
I will avoid discussing cache keys because the CDN vendor handles that.
I will disable browser caching so only one layer exists.
questions.explanation: Strong answers deliberately separate data classes and layer responsibilities.

questions.question: What trade-off often comes with aggressive edge caching of mutable public content?
options: Much better latency and origin offload, but more purge, freshness, and propagation complexity.
No extra complexity because public content never changes.
Lower privacy risk than no-store responses.
Guaranteed monotonic reads across every browser and region.
questions.explanation: Mutable public content benefits from edge caching, but freshness control becomes part of the design.
